items.q: What determines whether a timber is a hardwood or softwood?
items.correct: The type of tree - deciduous (hardwood) or coniferous (softwood), not its hardness.
items.misconception: Whether the wood itself is physically hard or soft.
items.random_wrong: How long the tree took to grow.
items.why: Students assume 'hardwood' means hard. Balsa is a hardwood but very soft; yew is a softwood but harder than many hardwoods. Classification is BOTANICAL, not based on hardness.

items.q: What defines a ferrous metal?
items.correct: It contains iron.
items.misconception: It is magnetic.
items.random_wrong: It is shiny.
items.why: Students use magnetism as the test, but stainless steel (ferrous) can be non-magnetic. The defining property is iron content - 'ferrous' comes from the Latin for iron.

items.q: Why can thermoplastics be recycled but thermosets cannot?
items.correct: Thermoplastics soften when heated and can be remoulded; thermosets have cross-links that lock the shape permanently.
items.misconception: Thermosets are too dangerous to recycle.
items.random_wrong: Thermoplastics are cheaper, so it is worth recycling them.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Thermosets undergo a one-way curing reaction creating cross-links; reheating them just degrades them without remelting. Thermoplastics have no cross-links so they remelt freely.

items.q: What is an iterative design process?
items.correct: A cyclical process where designs are repeatedly tested, evaluated, and refined.
items.misconception: A linear process from brief to final product.
items.random_wrong: A process used only for software.
items.why: Students learn a 'step 1, step 2' sequence and apply it to iteration too. Iteration loops back - you might redesign after testing, then test again, many times.

items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.q: Which of the 6 Rs has the GREATEST environmental benefit?
items.correct: Refuse or Reduce (preventing the impact at source).
items.misconception: Recycle (because it is most familiar).
items.random_wrong: Repair (because it sounds eco-friendly).
items.why: Students treat recycling as the gold standard, but recycling uses energy and downgrades materials. The hierarchy is Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle, Rethink - PREVENTION beats reprocessing.

items.q: Why is injection moulding suited to high-volume production?
items.correct: Tooling cost is high but per-unit cost is very low at scale.
items.misconception: It is the cheapest method for any quantity.
items.random_wrong: It is the only way to make plastic items.
items.why: Students think a 'good' process is good for everything. Injection moulding has expensive moulds - it's only economical when costs are spread over thousands or millions of parts.

items.q: If voltage is doubled and resistance is unchanged, what happens to the current?
items.correct: Current doubles (V = IR, so I = V/R).
items.misconception: Current halves.
items.random_wrong: Current stays the same.
items.why: Students get the direction of proportionality wrong. Current is PROPORTIONAL to voltage (when R is constant) - more push, more flow. They sometimes confuse this with current being inversely proportional to resistance.

items.q: What property do shape memory alloys have?
items.correct: They return to a pre-set shape when heated.
items.misconception: They remember every shape they have ever been bent into.
items.random_wrong: They glow when touched.
items.why: Students think SMAs are infinitely programmable. They actually have ONE 'remembered' shape, set during high-temperature treatment. Heating restores that shape; the alloy doesn't store a history of deformations.

items.q: Why do designers often use 5th to 95th percentile data instead of just the 50th?
items.correct: To accommodate the majority of users rather than just the average.
items.misconception: Because the 50th percentile is too unreliable.
items.random_wrong: Because law requires it.
items.why: Students think designing for the average user is best, but it excludes most people. The 5th-95th range covers 90% of users - designing only for the mean fails everyone above or below it.

items.q: What is the difference between a wood stain and a varnish?
items.correct: Stain colours the wood; varnish provides a protective surface coat.
items.misconception: They are interchangeable.
items.random_wrong: Stain is permanent, varnish always peels off.
items.why: Students confuse colouring (stain penetrates) with protecting (varnish sits on top). Many products combine both, but their primary functions differ. Often stain is applied first, then varnish.

items.q: What gives carbon fibre composite its strength?
items.correct: The fibres provide tensile strength; the resin matrix holds them in place and transfers loads.
items.misconception: The carbon fibres alone - the resin is just glue.
items.random_wrong: The black colour absorbing energy.
items.why: Students dismiss the matrix as filler. In reality the matrix is essential: it transfers loads between fibres and protects them. Loose carbon fibres without matrix have very little structural strength.

items.q: What does 'Rethink' mean in the 6 Rs?
items.correct: Questioning whether a product is necessary or could be designed differently for sustainability.
items.misconception: Trying a design again if it does not work first time.
items.random_wrong: Thinking about marketing the product.
items.why: Students apply 'rethink' to design iteration. In sustainability, it means challenging the NEED for a product altogether, or fundamentally rethinking how it's made - a more radical step than reducing materials.

items.q: What is batch production?
items.correct: Producing a specific quantity of identical items, then switching to make another batch.
items.misconception: Producing items continuously without stopping.
items.random_wrong: Producing one unique item.
items.why: Students confuse batch with mass production. Batch produces FIXED QUANTITIES with the line then changing - between one-off (single item) and mass (continuous high volume).

items.q: What force does a rope in a tug-of-war experience?
items.correct: Tension - it is being pulled at both ends.
items.misconception: Compression - because it is squeezed by hands.
items.random_wrong: Shear.
items.why: Students confuse the localised hand grip with the force through the rope. Pulling stretches the rope - that's tension. Compression would be pushing the ends together (which a rope cannot resist).

items.q: What is the difference between ductility and malleability?
items.correct: Ductility is being drawn into wires; malleability is being hammered into sheets.
items.misconception: They mean exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Ductility is for metals, malleability is for plastics.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms because both involve deformation. Ductility = tensile (stretching into wires); malleability = compressive (rolling/hammering into sheets). Some metals excel at one but not the other.

items.q: What is the purpose of a jig in manufacturing?
items.correct: To guide a tool and ensure consistent, accurate repeated operations.
items.misconception: To hold the workpiece very tightly so it does not move.
items.random_wrong: To inspect finished products.
items.why: Students confuse jigs with fixtures. A fixture HOLDS the workpiece; a jig GUIDES the tool (e.g., a drill jig guides the drill bit to the correct position) - though the terms are sometimes used loosely.

items.q: What is an orthographic drawing?
items.correct: A drawing showing multiple 2D views (front, side, plan) of an object.
items.misconception: A 3D drawing showing the object at an angle.
items.random_wrong: A drawing in black ink only.
items.why: Students confuse orthographic (multi-view 2D) with isometric (single 3D view). Orthographic projects each face flat onto separate views - used for engineering specification.

items.q: What does an LDR's resistance do as light intensity INCREASES?
items.correct: It decreases.
items.misconception: It increases (more light = more resistance).
items.random_wrong: It stays constant.
items.why: Students assume 'more input = more resistance', but LDR resistance is INVERSELY proportional to light. Dark = high resistance; bright = low resistance. This is why LDRs detect darkness in circuits.

items.q: What is a microcontroller?
items.correct: A small integrated chip containing CPU, memory, and I/O for controlling a device.
items.misconception: A very small computer like a laptop.
items.random_wrong: A remote control for a TV.
items.why: Students think microcontrollers are mini-PCs. They are specialised: limited resources, designed for a single embedded task. No operating system needed - just dedicated code running directly.

items.q: What is FEA used for in product design?
items.correct: Simulating how a product will respond to forces, heat, or vibration before manufacture.
items.misconception: Final exam analysis of student designs.
items.random_wrong: Counting the elements in a chemical compound.
items.why: Students unfamiliar with the acronym guess at it. FEA = Finite Element Analysis: dividing a part into many small elements and simulating physical behaviour computationally - saves prototyping costs.

items.q: What is inclusive design?
items.correct: Designing products usable by the widest possible range of people without special adaptation.
items.misconception: Designing only for disabled users.
items.random_wrong: Adding multiple language options to packaging.
items.why: Students conflate inclusive with specialist design. Inclusive design widens MAINSTREAM products to work for more people - the OXO Good Grips kitchen tools, designed for arthritic hands, work better for everyone.
